summary: My father's great-great-grandfather was originally from Germany, where he worked as a tutor. He left Germany with his wife and two sons, likely in the mid to late eighteenth century, seeking a new life in the United States. Unfortunately, their journey was tragic as their ship was wrecked off the coast of Maryland, and the parents were lost at sea.

The story goes that my ancestors' ship was wrecked off the coast of Maryland, where my great-great-grandfather's wife and he were lost. The two sons survived and reached shore in Maryland, but were destitute and had to bind themselves out as was customary then. Later, my grandfather and his brother lived in Allegheny County, Maryland before moving westward to Ohio in the early 1830s.

My family’s journey to the United States began with my great-great-grandfather leaving Germany with his family in the eighteenth century. Despite the tragedy at sea, the two boys made it to American shores and started their lives here under difficult circumstances. Eventually, my family settled and prospered, buying land in Ohio that was owned by the U.S. Government, with a deed signed by President Andrew Jackson.

In the early 1830s, my grandfather Myer and his brother moved from Maryland to Licking County, Ohio. In 1834, they purchased farmland on the banks of what is now Buckeye Lake, marking the start of our family's roots in that area. This land purchase was significant and remains in the family to this day, passed down through generations.

The farmland my grandfather bought in 1834 was located on the banks of what is now known as Buckeye Lake in Ohio. This land was originally owned by the U.S. Government and the deed was signed by President Andrew Jackson. A portion of this land still belongs to my family, specifically the estate of John Hyson Myer, and the original deed remains a treasured keepsake.

President Andrew Jackson's signature is on the sheepskin deed for the farmland my grandfather purchased in Ohio in 1834. This connection to a U.S. President adds a special historical significance to our family land. It reminds me of the early days of our settlement and the government’s role in opening land for pioneers like my family.

John Hyson Myer is my father, and the land purchased by my grandfather in 1834 is now part of his estate. He is the third generation to own this land by Buckeye Lake, Ohio, and the original deed signed by Andrew Jackson is still kept as a family heirloom. The land and legacy have been passed down through him and continue to hold great importance to our family.

My grandmother, Mary Oldaker, was born in Virginia in 1818, specifically in the upper part of the Shenandoah Valley. She spent her early years there before moving with her family to Ohio by horseback when she was still a girl. Virginia was the starting point of her journey westward, which shaped much of our family's history.

Mary Oldaker, my grandmother, was born in the upper part of the Shenandoah Valley, which is now part of West Virginia. This region was her childhood home before her family moved to Ohio by horseback. The Shenandoah Valley holds a special place in our family history as the origin of my grandmother’s lineage.

The area where my grandmother Mary Oldaker was born, the upper Shenandoah Valley, is now part of West Virginia. Although she was born in 1818 when it was Virginia, the region later became West Virginia. This geographical change marks an interesting historical context for our family’s origins.

My grandmother, Mary Oldaker, was born in 1818 in the upper Shenandoah Valley and moved to Ohio as a young girl by horseback with her family. Her father was a millwright who traveled to build mills, which likely influenced her upbringing. She married my grandfather Jacob Myer at age forty-two and was the mother of my father, born when she was forty-three.

Jacob Myer was my grandfather and the husband of my grandmother Mary Oldaker. He was older than Mary and she was his second wife. Jacob died in 1866 when my father was only five years old, leaving Mary a widow with a young son and a farm to manage.
